Name: Le Carmin
What_to_order: the market tasting menu — the kitchen does not run a fixed signature; lunch 50–59 €, dinner menus 118 / 148 / 178 €
Notes: Chef Christophe Quéant, trained with Robuchon and Ducasse. An Henri-IV house on the central square, the menu rotating strictly with the market. Closed Sun, Mon, Tue lunch.

Name: Clos du Cèdre
What_to_order: the seasonal tasting menu — terroir-anchored Burgundian, a wine list past 1,000 references
Notes: Chef Jordan Billan, inside the Hostellerie Cèdre & Spa under the great cedar that names it. Lunch Sat–Sun only; dinner Wed–Sun. Smart dress.

Name: Ma Cuisine
Recognition: The reference Burgundian table in Beaune, since 1996
What_to_order: escargots à la bourguignonne, bœuf bourguignon, roast pigeon — à la carte only, no set menu
Notes: A discreet table down a passage, beloved for its cellar — the wine list opens around 50 € and runs very deep. Reopened February 2026 under chef Mathieu Escoffier. Dinner only, closed Sunday.

Name: Bissoh
Recognition: The Japanese-Burgundian counter — sumibi-yaki on binchotan charcoal
What_to_order: aged Limousin and Miyazaki beef from the charcoal grill; the omakase counter
Notes: Chef Mikihiko Sawahata cooks in front of ten seats. A very large French wine list behind a Japanese grill. Wed–Sun dinner; closed Mon–Tue.

Name: Le Bistro de l'Hôtel
Recognition: House table of the five-star Hôtel le Cep
What_to_order: whole roast poularde de Bresse for two; Charolais côte de bœuf
Notes: A small panelled room doing one thing supremely — the whole Bresse hen, carved at the table. The address for poultry without driving to the Bresse.

Name: Caves Madeleine
Recognition: Bistrot with the bones of a wine cellar — shared tables
What_to_order: hanger-steak tartare with hazelnut and Parmesan; the producer-sourced plat du jour
Notes: Long communal tables, wine bought off the shelf at retail-plus, kitchen that cooks with real punch. Closed Wed and Sun.

Name: Le Bistrot Bourguignon
Recognition: The honest Burgundian bouchon — Menu Bourguignon 33 €
What_to_order: œufs en meurette, bœuf bourguignon, andouillette sauce moutarde, entrecôte sauce Époisses
Notes: Chef Mickael Thibert; a wine list past 500 references in a small room. The canon dishes done straight, no folklore. Closed Sun–Mon.

Name: La Table du Square
What_to_order: the market menu — rewritten every fortnight
Notes: Chef Romain Escoffier. The best value-for-craft in Beaune by Michelin's own reckoning, a wine list past 1,000 labels. Closed Sun–Mon.

Name: Loiseau des Vignes
Recognition: Bernard Loiseau group — the wine-by-the-glass bistro
What_to_order: œufs en meurette; pâté en croûte; 32 Burgundies poured by the glass from four oak cellars
Notes: The Saulieu three-star group's Beaune address. The point is the glass list — 32 wines kept properly, the way to drink across the Côte in one sitting. Its gougères are a benchmark. Closed Sun–Mon.

Name: Le Comptoir des Tontons
What_to_order: the seasonal organic market plates; taste a wine, then buy the bottle off the shelf
Notes: A small room of natural-wine conviction on the Madeleine faubourg. Reserve — there are not many seats.

Name: Enstase
Recognition: Cave à manger — the natural-wine address of Beaune
What_to_order: natural wine, cider and kefir; Thursday and Friday the kitchen opens — bao and elaborate small plates
Notes: A room built entirely of natural materials, a low-intervention wine list, and a kitchen that wakes up at the week's end. The counter where young Beaune drinks.

Name: Marché aux Vins
Recognition: Self-guided tasting cellar in the former Cordeliers church
What_to_order: Découverte (5 wines) 25 € or Prestige (7 wines) 49 € — taste as you walk the chapels
Notes: Three intact Gothic chapels turned tasting cellar, vintages stored 40 years underground. Go at opening, 10:00 on a weekday, when the stone is cold and empty.

Name: Patriarche Père & Fils
Recognition: The largest cellars in Burgundy — 5 km of galleries
What_to_order: the self-guided cellar visit with a tasting of 6 wines, 25 €
Notes: Founded 1780, the galleries dug under a former Visitandine convent — around two million bottles ageing in the dark. The clearest sense of how much wine sleeps beneath Beaune. Verify hours before going.

Name: Bouchard Père & Fils
What_to_order: the guided fortress-cellar visit and tasting; the Beaune Grèves 'Vigne de l'Enfant Jésus'
Notes: Bouchard took the Château de Beaune — built for Louis XI — in 1820; the cellars run 10 m into the rampart, holding 19th-century bottles. Book ahead; tours Tue–Sat.

Name: Marché des Halles (Saturday)
Neighborhood: Place de la Halle & Place Fleury
Recognition: The Saturday market — around 180 stalls, a real local event
What_to_order: Époisses and Cîteaux cheese; jambon persillé; Fallot mustard from the stand; autumn girolles
Notes: 07:00–13:00 across Place de la Halle, Place Fleury and the covered halls. Saturday is the big one; Wednesday is the quieter, food-only market. Producers down from the hill villages.

Name: Pâtisserie Fabien Berteau
What_to_order: the seasonal-fruit cheesecake, the chef's signature; macarons; the morning viennoiserie
Notes: Revised French classics and house-made ice cream. The tea room Le Garden opposite does an affordable lunch under a shaded summer terrace. Closed Wednesday.
